title: 10-year compliance vault
subtitle: Timestamped, signed evidence archives for long-term retention

what.title: What is the compliance vault?
what.sub: Long-term evidence archive required by CRA Article 13(10)
what.body:
The compliance vault creates timestamped, digitally signed archives of your product's compliance evidence. These archives are designed to satisfy CRA Article 13(10), which requires manufacturers to retain technical documentation and the EU Declaration of Conformity for at least 10 years after the product is placed on the market, or for the duration of the support period, whichever is longer.

Each archive is called a compliance snapshot. A snapshot captures the complete state of your product's compliance evidence at a specific point in time and packages it into a signed ZIP file.

  • Immutable – once generated, a snapshot cannot be modified
  • Timestamped – RFC 3161 trusted timestamp from an independent Time Stamping Authority
  • Signed – Ed25519 digital signature (with ML-DSA-65 post-quantum signature where available) using CRANIS2's published signing keys
  • Verifiable – anyone can verify the signature using the public key at /.well-known/cranis2-signing-key.pem
The vault is available on all plans. Generating snapshots, downloading archives, and managing retention schedules does not require a Pro plan.

generate.title: Generate a compliance snapshot
generate.sub: Capture the current state of compliance evidence
generate.body:
Navigate to your product's Evidence tab group and select the Compliance Vault tab. Click Generate Snapshot to create a new archive.

Snapshot generation runs in the background. The status progresses from generating to complete (or failed if an error occurs). You can poll the status or refresh the page – most snapshots complete within 30 seconds.

The platform records the following for each snapshot:

  • Creation timestamp and the user who triggered generation
  • SHA-256 content hash of the archive (for integrity verification)
  • File size in bytes
  • RFC 3161 trusted timestamp (if available)
  • Ed25519 + ML-DSA-65 digital signatures (if signing keys are configured)
Generate a snapshot at every significant compliance milestone: before market placement, after each major release, after resolving critical vulnerabilities, and whenever the Technical File is substantially updated. This creates a chronological evidence trail that auditors can follow.

Reversibility: snapshots can be deleted individually. Deletion removes the local file and any cold storage copy. The retention ledger records the deletion event for audit purposes.

contents.title: What the snapshot contains
contents.sub: Complete evidence package for regulatory audit
contents.body:
Each compliance snapshot is a ZIP archive containing all evidence required by CRA Annex VII:

  • Technical File – all eight Annex VII sections (product description, design and development, vulnerability handling, risk assessment, support period, standards applied, test reports, declaration of conformity) as structured JSON and human-readable Markdown
  • SBOM – Software Bill of Materials in SPDX JSON and CycloneDX JSON formats, listing all dependencies with versions and licences
  • Vulnerability report – current vulnerability findings with severity, status, and triage decisions
  • Licence audit – licence compliance analysis for all dependencies
  • Obligation status – the status of all applicable CRA obligations for the product's category and role
  • Stakeholder contacts – registered compliance contacts (manufacturer representative, security contact, CVD policy URL)
  • Product metadata – name, version, CRA category, distribution model, repository connection, support period dates
The snapshot captures the state of evidence at the moment of generation. If you update your Technical File or resolve vulnerabilities after generating a snapshot, those changes will appear in the next snapshot, not in the existing one. This is by design – each snapshot is a point-in-time record.

integrity.title: Cryptographic integrity verification
integrity.sub: How snapshots are signed, timestamped, and verified
integrity.body:
Every compliance snapshot is protected by multiple layers of cryptographic integrity:

SHA-256 content hash. The archive's content is hashed at generation time. This hash is stored in the database and can be compared against the downloaded file to confirm it has not been altered.

Ed25519 digital signature. The archive is signed with CRANIS2's Ed25519 signing key. The corresponding public key is published at /.well-known/cranis2-signing-key.pem for independent verification. The signature file (.sig) is stored alongside the archive.

ML-DSA-65 post-quantum signature. Where available, a second signature is generated using the NIST-standardised ML-DSA-65 algorithm. This provides protection against future quantum computing attacks on classical signatures. The post-quantum public key is at /.well-known/cranis2-signing-key-mldsa.pem.

RFC 3161 trusted timestamp. An independent Time Stamping Authority provides a cryptographic proof of when the archive was created. This establishes the existence of the evidence at a specific point in time, independent of CRANIS2's own systems.

The combination of content hashing, dual digital signatures, and an independent trusted timestamp creates a chain of evidence that satisfies the most rigorous audit requirements. Market surveillance authorities can independently verify that evidence existed and was unmodified since the claimed creation date.

manage.title: Download, schedule, and manage snapshots
manage.sub: Export archives and configure automatic generation
manage.body:
The vault tab shows all snapshots for a product, ordered by creation date. For each snapshot you can:

  • Download – download the signed ZIP archive to your local system or a corporate archive
  • View details – see the content hash, signatures, timestamp, file size, and who generated it
  • Delete – remove the snapshot (the deletion is recorded in the retention ledger)

Scheduled snapshots. You can configure automatic snapshot generation on a schedule (e.g. weekly, monthly, or after each release). Navigate to the schedule settings to define the frequency and the products to include. Scheduled snapshots generate in the background and appear in the vault like manual snapshots.

Cold storage. Snapshots are automatically uploaded to cold storage (where configured) for disaster recovery. If the primary storage is lost, cold storage copies can be restored. The cold storage location is recorded in the retention ledger.

The retention ledger (accessible from the admin panel) records every vault event: snapshot generation, download, deletion, cold storage upload, and retention certificate issuance. This ledger is itself an immutable audit trail that demonstrates your organisation's compliance documentation management.

done.title: Using the vault effectively
done.sub: Practical guidance for long-term compliance evidence management
done.body:
The compliance vault is most effective when used as a routine part of your release and compliance workflow. Recommended practices:

  • Pre-market snapshot. Before placing a product on the EU market, generate a snapshot that captures the complete Technical File, SBOM, vulnerability assessment, and Declaration of Conformity. This is your baseline evidence for CRA Article 13(10).
  • Post-release snapshots. After each significant release, generate a new snapshot. This creates a versioned evidence trail showing how compliance evidence evolved over the product lifecycle.
  • Post-remediation snapshots. After resolving critical or high-severity vulnerabilities, generate a snapshot to demonstrate that the product was returned to a compliant state. This supports CRA Article 13(6) (vulnerability handling) evidence.
  • Annual review. Generate at least one snapshot per year during the support period to demonstrate ongoing compliance monitoring, even if no major changes have occurred.
When preparing for an audit or market surveillance inquiry, export the relevant snapshots and provide them alongside the signature files and public keys. The auditor can independently verify the content hash and digital signatures without needing access to CRANIS2.

Snapshots are retained for the duration documented in the Privacy Policy. The 10-year retention period required by CRA Article 13(10) is measured from the date of market placement or the end of the support period, whichever is later.

schedule.title: Automatic snapshot scheduling
schedule.body:
Snapshot schedules allow you to automate evidence generation without manual intervention. This ensures compliance evidence is captured at regular intervals throughout the product's support period.

How to configure:

  • Navigate to the product's Compliance Vault tab
  • Click Schedule Settings
  • Choose a frequency: weekly, fortnightly, monthly, or quarterly
  • Select which products to include in the schedule
  • Save the configuration

Scheduled snapshots are generated automatically by the platform's background scheduler. They appear in the vault alongside manual snapshots and carry the same cryptographic protections (hash, signature, timestamp).

Monthly snapshots are a good default for most products. Increase the frequency to weekly during periods of active development or vulnerability remediation. Reduce to quarterly for stable products in maintenance mode.

ledger.title: Retention reserve ledger
ledger.sub: Financial and operational audit trail for vault activities
ledger.body:
The retention reserve ledger is an immutable record of all vault operations across your organisation. It is accessible from the Admin panel under Retention Ledger.

The ledger records:

  • Snapshot generation – product, timestamp, file size, content hash, cold storage key
  • Retention certificates – cryptographically signed certificates confirming the existence and integrity of archived evidence
  • Cold storage uploads – confirmation that the archive was replicated to disaster recovery storage
  • Snapshot deletion – who deleted it and when
  • Funding reserve calculations – estimated storage costs over the 10-year retention period
The ledger satisfies the record-keeping aspect of CRA Article 13(10) by providing an independent, tamper-resistant log that your organisation maintained compliance documentation throughout the required retention period. This is distinct from the compliance snapshots themselves – the ledger tracks the management of evidence, while the snapshots contain the evidence.
